THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY GORDON ECKERT MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR WILL DOUBLE EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS TRENDS HAS DRIVEN THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING TECHNOLOGY FOR DECADES. HOWEVER, AS WE APPROACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON BASED TECHNOLOGY, IT BECOMES CLEAR THAT THE LAW MAY EVENTUALLY CEASE TO HOLD TRUE. FACTORS SUCH AS HEAT DISSIPATION, QUANTUM TUNNELLING, AND THE COST OF MANUFACTURING SMALLER COMPONENTS ARE BECOMING SIGNIFICANT BARRIERS TO FURTHER MINIATURIZATION.

***
WHILE THE EXACT TIME WHEN THE LAW OF MOORE WILL CEASE TO APPLY IS DIFFICULT TO PREDICT, MANY EXPERTS BELIEVE IT MAY BE AROUND 2030 TO 2040. HOWEVER, THIS DOES NOT MEAN THE END OF TECHNOLOGICAL PROGRESS. INSTEAD, IT MAY LEAD TO NEW INNOVATIONS SUCH AS QUANTUM COMPUTING, NEURAL NETWORKS, AND ADVANCED MATERIALS THAT CAN OVERCOME THE LIMITATIONS OF SILICON BASED TECHNOLOGY. THE FUTURE OF COMPUTING WILL LIKELY INVOLVE A SHIFT TOWARD DIFFERENT TYPES OF COMPUTING ARCHITECTURES AND MATERIALS RATHER THAN A CONTINUATION OF THE SAME TRENDS.
